title: The Bouncer’s Playbook
subtitle: Controls, false positives & the friction balance

hook.metaphor: A great bouncer keeps the troublemakers out *without* making the whole queue miserable. Check everyone too hard and the genuine customers leave; wave everyone through and the criminals stroll in. The art of running a financial-crime programme is exactly this balancing act — security versus a smooth experience — and getting the balance wrong has real victims on both sides.
hook.scenario: A bank's fraud system is set so tight it blocks thousands of legitimate payments a day ('false positives'), furious customers flood the call centre, and overwhelmed analysts start rubber-stamping alerts — so a real fraud slips through. Tightening the net made them *less* safe. How?

learn: The defences build on each other: **prevention** (KYC at the door, strong authentication), **detection** (transaction monitoring, sanctions screening, anomaly models), and **response** (investigate, freeze, report). Same preventive/detective/corrective logic as Operational Risk — applied to crime.

The central tension is **friction vs. experience.** Every check adds friction. Too little and criminals get through; too much and you punish honest customers, drive them away, and — as in our scenario — *overload your own analysts* until they miss the real thing. More alerts is not more safety.

Which is why **false positives** are the quiet enemy. Most alerts are innocent; if analysts drown in noise, genuine threats hide in the flood. The goal isn't *maximum* alerts, it's *smart* alerts — increasingly tuned with the AI/ML from the Credit-Risk track (and carrying the same bias and explainability risks). And beware over-correcting into **de-risking** — bluntly dumping whole categories of customers to avoid effort, which just pushes vulnerable people out of the banking system.

Wrapping it all is **governance** (Credit-Risk Level 6, again): a named accountable owner (often an **MLRO** — Money Laundering Reporting Officer), regular independent review, staff training, and a culture that rewards surfacing suspicions. Regulators like **AUSTRAC**, the **RBNZ/DIA**, **FIU-IND** and others don't just want controls to *exist* — they want evidence they actually *work*, with eye-watering fines for firms that treat it as a box-tick.
bridge: So at work, you can judge a financial-crime programme by more than 'do they have controls?' Ask: *Is the friction calibrated — catching criminals without drowning honest customers and analysts? Are false positives managed, or is everyone numb to alerts? And is there real governance and evidence it works?* The best programmes are precise, not just strict.

interactions.title: Why did tightening backfire?
interactions.prompt: Cranking the fraud rules to maximum led to a real fraud slipping through. The core lesson?

options.label: They didn't tighten enough — go even stricter.
options.feedback: Stricter would worsen it. The flood of false positives is what numbed the analysts. Beyond a point, more alerts means less real safety.

options.label: Over-alerting drowned analysts; smarter, tuned detection beats blunt strictness.
options.feedback: Exactly. Security isn't a volume dial. Too many false positives exhausts your people and hides real threats. Precision, not maximum friction, is the goal.

options.label: Abolish the controls — they clearly don't work.
options.feedback: The opposite error. The controls weren't wrong to exist; they were mis-tuned. You calibrate the balance, you don't remove the net.

interactions.title: Prevent, detect, or respond?
interactions.prompt: Sort each financial-crime control by its role.

items.label: KYC checks before opening an account
Transaction monitoring flagging an odd pattern
Freezing an account and filing a SAR
Sanctions screening catching a banned name

interactions.title: Running the programme
interactions.prompt: Match each concept to its meaning.

pairs.right: An alert that turns out to be innocent
Bluntly dropping whole customer groups to avoid effort
The accountable owner of the AML programme
Matching the depth of checks to the level of risk

teachBack: Explain it to a friend in one line: “Fighting financial crime is a balancing act — enough friction to stop criminals, not so much that you drown honest customers and your own analysts in false positives — all held together by real governance.”

checkpoint.q: Why can cranking fraud controls to 'maximum' make a firm *less* safe?
options: It never can — stricter is always safer
Floods of false positives overload analysts so real threats slip through
checkpoint.explain: Beyond a point, more alerts numb your people and hide genuine threats — precision beats blunt strictness.

options: Bluntly dumping whole categories of customers to avoid effort
checkpoint.explain: It's the lazy over-correction — excluding entire groups, often pushing vulnerable people out of banking.

checkpoint.q: Regulators like AUSTRAC and FIU-IND primarily want to see that controls…
options: Actually work, with evidence and accountable ownership
Are as strict as technically possible
checkpoint.explain: Box-ticking isn't enough — they expect demonstrable effectiveness, governance, and an accountable owner (MLRO).
